quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: You made it back. Two of my couriers didn't — found them on the Via Valeria with their throats cut and the dispatches still in their boots. Whoever killed them couldn't read Marsic. The Lex Julia has passed — citizenship for any Italian community that surrenders within sixty days. The Paeligni have accepted. The Vestini. Half my own council wants to take it. The Samnites say they'll die first. I have three days before this alliance fractures beyond mending. So — you came back to advise me. Advise me.

options.playerDialogue: Take it. It's not everything — but it's the thing your people have been dying for. Don't let the Samnites' war become your war.
Don't take it. The moment you disarm, they'll gut the terms. You have leverage now — you won't have it again.
Accept the citizenship publicly — send the delegation, make the speeches. But keep the veterans under arms until the tribal enrollment is complete. Not as a threat. As a fact.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: The thing my people have been dying for. Yes. And also the thing my people have been dying for — do you hear the difference? Gaius Vettius — he held the bridge at Fucine Lake for three hours so the column could cross. Dead. His son enlisted the next morning. Also dead. I cannot go to their mother and say we stopped one battle short of what they purchased. But — the Lex Julia is more than any generation before us received. The Samnites will call me a Roman dog for taking it.

responses.playerDialogue: Let the Samnites fight alone. They want independence — you want citizenship. Those were never the same war.
The mother of those boys — what would she say? Not what the Samnites want. What she wants.
Tell the Samnites plainly — the Marsi fought for a seat at the table, not to burn the house down. You got the seat. Sit in it.
Don't frame it as surrender — frame it as phase one. The Marsi accept peace, build political power inside the Republic, and become the allies the Samnites will need in ten years.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I can't tell you what those deaths were worth. But I can tell you what I've seen in Rome — the appetite for this war is gone.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: A Roman telling me to keep killing Romans. I've been trying to decide if that makes you the most honest man I've spoken to or the most dangerous. The Samnites would embrace you for saying it — Papius Mutilus has been arguing exactly this since the Lex Julia was announced. But my people, Roman — my people are tired in a way you can't understand. They've buried their sons in winter ground. They want to go home to fields that haven't been planted in two seasons. I can ask the Marsi to die for freedom. I cannot ask them to die for leverage.

options.playerDialogue: Then is there something between surrender and war? Something that keeps your people alive and your position intact?

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: Accept and keep the swords. You think like us — not like a Roman. Yes. That is exactly what I've been considering. Rome is tearing itself apart — Sulla and Marius circling each other like dogs over a corpse. The legions are scattered between our front and their own civil fracture. If we accept now, they can't afford to disarm us while fighting themselves. By the time they turn their attention back to the Marsi, we'll be citizens with veteran armies — and legal ones. The question is making the acceptance look genuine.

options.playerDialogue: I can help with that. A Roman vouching for Marsic good faith buys you time — and costs me something, which makes it believable.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Between surrender and war. My father used to say the Marsi have always lived in that country — the narrow ground between what Rome offers and what Rome means. Let me think.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Here is what I need — and I'm not negotiating, I'm telling you the minimum my council will accept. The Marsi accept the Lex Julia publicly, with ceremony — I'll even wear a toga if it helps Rome save face. In return, three things. The Lex Varia prosecutions end — every Italian convicted under it walks free with property restored. Marsic veterans receive land grants on the same terms as Roman legionaries — including on land they already farm. And the tribal enrollment happens now. Not in five years. Not when it's convenient. Now. Can you carry those terms, or are they just more words for your jar?

options.playerDialogue: I'll carry them to Sulpicius — he has the tribunician power and the nerve to use it. That's not a promise. That's a plan.
I'll carry them — but I won't lie to you about who's in power when I arrive. Rome is an open question right now.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: I have trusted exactly one Roman in my life and he died for it. I'm going to trust a second. I will accept the Lex Julia for the Marsi — and I will name you as the Roman guarantor of the peace. Do you understand what that means? It means if Rome breaks its word — if the enrollment is delayed, if the prosecutions resume, if a single Marsic veteran is denied his land — your name bears the dishonor alongside mine. My people will hold you to it. So will I.

options.playerDialogue: My name is on it. If Rome dishonors this peace, I'll stand in the Forum and say so myself.
In private — yes, absolutely. But I can't sign a public document. Not with proscription lists circulating in Rome.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: An open question. Your Republic doesn't even know who governs it — and you came here to advise me. There's something almost admirable in that. Go home, Roman. I'll decide for the Marsi the way we've always decided — by ourselves, on our own ground, without waiting for Rome to tell us what we're permitted to want. We were a people before you were a city. We'll manage.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: Then it's done — not with a triumph, not with a surrender, but with two men sitting in a tent agreeing that enough boys have died. My people will remember your name. We carve the names of friends into the lintels of our houses — so that everyone who enters knows who was welcome there. I will carve yours. Go safely. And keep your word — it's the only Roman thing I've ever found worth keeping.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Private guarantees. Whispered promises. Rome's oldest export — older than wine, older than law. I'll take what I can get. The Marsi will accept the Lex Julia. We'll smile at the ceremony. And we'll sleep with our swords under our beds, the way my grandfather did, and his grandfather before him. Tell your Senate we are peaceful. Don't tell them we are trusting. Those have never been the same thing.

operations.reason: Named guarantor of the Marsic peace
The populares claim the peace as a reform victory
Conservative senators view the peace terms as too generous

deltas.reason: Chose peace over war at personal risk

operations.reason: The Marsi accept the Lex Julia with reservations

deltas.reason: Negotiated a deal without fully committing to either side

operations.reason: Poppaedius no longer trusts Roman intermediaries

effects.reason: The failed negotiation costs you credibility and time
